nativeRange.habitat: Found at the ocean surface — and occasionally down to around 50 m — drifting among floating mats of Sargassum seaweed and other open-ocean debris. This surface-drifting lifestyle, sometimes called pelagic-neustonic, is unique among frogfishes.

diet.notes: Ambush predator that wiggles a modified first dorsal spine (illicium) tipped with a fleshy esca to lure small fish, shrimp, and other crustaceans. Has been documented expanding its mouth via suction feeding to engulf prey equal to or larger than its own body length. New specimens generally accept only live feeder fish or shrimp, though many can be trained over time onto frozen krill, silversides, or pieces of marine fish offered from tongs. Feeding every 4–6 days is commonly advised — overfeeding has been reported to cause bloat and feeding refusal.

goodWith: larger peaceful fish too big to be swallowed
avoidWith: aggressive nippers that target the lure

summary: The Sargassum Fish (Histrio histrio) is a small pelagic frogfish that drifts with open-ocean mats of Sargassum weed, using a modified dorsal spine to lure prey. It is an ambush predator capable of swallowing fish equal to its own body length and must be housed with care.
careNotes:
The Sargassum Fish (Histrio histrio) is the only member of its genus and stands apart from most frogfishes by living at the ocean surface rather than on the reef floor. In the wild it drifts among floating mats of Sargassum seaweed and other open-ocean debris, where its laterally compressed body, irregular skin filaments, and mottled tan, brown, yellow, and green coloration allow it to disappear almost entirely against the weed. Individuals can shift hue over time to better match their immediate surroundings.

The fish hunts by wiggling a modified first dorsal spine — called an illicium — tipped with a fleshy lure called an esca that mimics a small prey item. When a target approaches, the fish strikes with one of the fastest documented feeding actions in vertebrates, expanding its mouth many times its resting volume by suction. It has been documented swallowing prey equal to or exceeding its own body length, and is widely reported to be cannibalistic toward other members of the same species, so solitary keeping is generally advised.

A tank of at least 200 litres (around 90 cm in length) is commonly recommended. Despite its modest adult size of 15–20 cm, the Sargassum Fish benefits from open horizontal swimming space to support its lure-and-strike hunting cycle. Floating macroalgae or sargassum-style decorations near the surface help replicate the species' natural environment.

Reef-safety requires some nuance. The Sargassum Fish tends to ignore corals and will not nip them, so it is broadly considered reef-compatible in that limited sense. However, it will prey on any fish or motile invertebrate small enough to fit in its mouth — ornamental shrimp, small wrasses, gobies, blennies, and cardinalfish are all at risk. It should NOT be housed with triggerfish or pufferfish, which have been reported to bite off the lure appendage.

Feeding is the principal husbandry challenge. Newly acquired specimens commonly accept only live prey, and starvation is a documented risk if a patient transition to frozen foods is not established. Many keepers train the fish onto frozen silversides or krill offered from feeding tongs, mimicking the motion of live prey.

Because this species has been reported to leap onto floating weed mats in the wild and can survive briefly out of water, a tight-fitting lid is generally advised. No venomous spines are documented.
breedingNotes: Oviparous. Females ascend toward the surface and release eggs bound together in a gelatinous mucus 'raft' or 'veil' that drifts among the sargassum until hatching; males fertilize the raft externally.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry sheet — sizing (30 gal min, 6 in max), water parameters (72–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), reef-compatible rating, carnivore diet, peaceful temperament rating, ambush-predator behavior.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Antennariidae, Lophiiformes), maximum length (20 cm TL), pelagic-neustonic habitat among floating Sargassum, global distribution (Atlantic + Indo-Pacific), depth range 0–50 m, preferred temperature 21.4–29.2 °C (mean 27.7 °C), oviparous reproduction with gelatinous egg raft, IUCN Least Concern. Replaces designated secondary (Reef2Reef thread returned 403).

additional.name: Wikipedia (Histrio histrio)
additional.notes: Cross-checked biology and behavior. Confirmed monotypic genus, max length 20 cm, lure morphology (illicium + esca), cannibalistic ambush predation with rapid suction strike, ability to leap above the surface onto weed mats and survive briefly out of water, gelatinous egg-raft reproduction.

additional.name: AquariumDomain (Sargassum Anglerfish)
additional.notes: Cross-checked hobbyist husbandry — 55 gal minimum, 72–80 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, semi-aggressive temperament, moderate care level, and the specific caution to avoid housing with puffers or triggers (which damage the lure appendage).

additional.name: Saltwaterfish.com (Sargassum Angler)
additional.notes: Triangulated tank-size (50 gal / ~190 L), water parameters (74–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025), aggressive temperament rating, and 3–5 year lifespan estimate.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with a gelatinous floating egg raft. Captive breeding is uncommon, and maintaining a pair is difficult given reports of cannibalism between individuals outside of spawning.
